book_claims.claim: 4+ servings per week of omega-3 rich fish lowers heart attack risk by 22%. Even 1-4 servings per month has significant cardioprotective effect.
book_claims.mechanism: EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids block impulses causing irregular heart rhythm (antiarrhythmic), lower triglycerides, decrease blood pressure and inflammation, decrease platelet aggregation, improve vascular function.

book_claims.claim: Broiled/baked fish 3+ times/week: 49% lower heart attack death risk and 58% lower sudden cardiac death risk. Fried fish provides NO protection and may increase risk.
book_claims.mechanism: Commercially fried seafood cooked in partially hydrogenated oils containing trans-fat negates benefits

book_claims.claim: Fish exceeds meat in vitamin B12 bioavailability and unlike red meat, consumption associated with lower colon cancer risk
book_claims.mechanism: High B12 bioavailability without the carcinogenic compounds in red meat

supplementary_research.finding: REDUCE-IT trial (Bhatt et al., 2019) demonstrated that high-dose EPA (icosapent ethyl 4g/day) reduced major adverse cardiovascular events by 25% in statin-treated patients with elevated triglycerides, confirming the cardioprotective mechanism of marine omega-3.

Prospective cohort study (Li et al., 2021) of 420,000 participants found 2+ servings/week of oily fish associated with 17% lower risk of sudden cardiac death, consistent with the antiarrhythmic mechanism described in the book.

Systematic review (Abdelhamid et al., Cochrane 2020) of 86 RCTs found moderate evidence that increasing EPA/DHA slightly reduces coronary heart disease events and mortality, with stronger effects at higher doses and in high-risk populations.

culinary_pairings.source: Acid cuts richness; vitamin C aids iron absorption
Classic herb pairing with salmon, herring, mackerel
Combined unsaturated fat profiles for cardiovascular synergy
Traditional preparation for sardines and mackerel
Rye bread with herring; rice with grilled mackerel

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Salmon: 1,200-2,400mg; anchovies: 2,300-2,400mg; sardines: 1,100-1,600mg; mackerel: 1,350-2,100mg per 115g serving.
Oily fish are the most significant natural dietary source of vitamin D3. Wild salmon contains substantially more than farmed.
Highly bioavailable selenomethionine form; essential cofactor for glutathione peroxidase.
